skill_areas.name: Grip Fundamentals
skill_areas.description: Choosing and mastering shakehand or penhold grip. Foundation for all strokes.
common_mistakes: Gripping the handle too tightly causing wrist stiffness
Thumb pressing flat on forehand rubber reducing backhand flexibility
Index finger too far up the blade affecting control
Not adjusting grip pressure between rallying and serving
pain_points: Hand cramps from overly tight grip
Cannot switch between forehand and backhand quickly
Racket twists in hand on off-center hits
drills: Grip pressure awareness drill — rally while consciously relaxing grip
Forehand-backhand transition with focus on finger pressure shift
Penhold grip exploration — practice basic drives with penhold

skill_areas.name: Serving
skill_areas.description: Master backspin, topspin, sidespin, and short serves with deception. Serves are the only shot you have 100% control over.
progression_path: Learn legal serve rules (open palm, 16cm toss)
Short serve (two bounces on opponent side)
common_mistakes: Hiding the ball during serve (illegal under ITTF rules)
Not tossing the ball high enough (must be 16cm+)
Using arm instead of wrist for spin generation
Telegraphing spin type with body position or toss angle
Serving too long, giving opponent easy attack
Contacting ball too high, making serve sit up
pain_points: Cannot generate enough spin on serves
Serve goes too long giving opponent easy attack
Lack of variation and deception
Illegal serve (hidden contact point)
Cannot keep backspin serves short
drills: Backspin serve into a target box (place towel on table)
Pendulum serve with spin variation — same motion, different spin
Short serve practice — ball must bounce twice on opponent side
Serve and 3rd ball attack drill — serve then attack the return
Ghost serve practice — no-spin with spin motion

skill_areas.name: Forehand Loop/Topspin
skill_areas.description: Generate heavy topspin attacks from mid-distance using forehand loop against backspin and topspin. The primary attacking weapon in modern table tennis.
progression_path: Loop against backspin (open bat, brush up)
Power loop vs spin loop variation
common_mistakes: Hitting flat instead of brushing up on the ball
Contacting ball too late (behind the body)
Not using legs and core — arm-only swing
Opening bat angle too much against topspin
Standing too far or too close to table
Not returning to ready position after the loop
pain_points: Inconsistent contact point
Not enough spin — hitting flat
Poor weight transfer and timing
Cannot loop heavy backspin
Loop goes into the net against backspin
drills: Multi-ball forehand loop drill (coach feeds backspin)
Loop against backspin push — alternate push and loop
Forehand loop crosscourt and down the line alternation
Loop from mid-distance stepping back and in
Counter-loop rally drill with partner

skill_areas.name: Backhand Techniques
skill_areas.description: Backhand drive, flick, loop, and punch techniques. Modern table tennis demands a strong backhand for close-to-table exchanges.
common_mistakes: Elbow too close to body restricting swing
Using shoulder instead of forearm and wrist
Bat angle too open causing ball to pop up
Not using core rotation — relying on arm only
Standing too square instead of slightly side-on
pain_points: Weak backhand compared to forehand
Cannot generate power on backhand loops
Backhand flick timing errors
Elbow positioning issues
Cannot sustain backhand-to-backhand rallies
drills: Backhand topspin rally drill — 50 consecutive
Backhand flick against short serve from partner
Forehand-backhand transition drill (Falkenberg variant)
Backhand loop against multiball backspin feed
Backhand counter-topspin at close range

skill_areas.name: Flick Techniques (Banana & Strawberry)
skill_areas.description: Aggressive receive options against short serves. The banana flick (backhand) and strawberry flick (reverse-spin) are essential modern weapons.
progression_path: Basic forehand flick over the table
Reading serve spin before choosing flick type
common_mistakes: Reaching in too late — ball already dropping below table level
Not getting close enough to the ball (footwork failure)
Flicking with arm instead of wrist snap
Using banana flick against long serves (wrong situation)
Not reading spin before committing to flick
pain_points: Cannot execute banana flick consistently
Flick goes off the end of the table
Timing feels rushed and panicked
Cannot read if serve is short enough to flick
drills: Partner serves short — practice banana flick receive
Slow-motion wrist snap drills without ball
Flick placement drill — crosscourt vs middle vs down the line
Alternating banana flick and push receive against serve

skill_areas.name: Pushing (Backspin Control)
skill_areas.description: Control backspin rallies with long push, short push, and heavy push. Pushing is the foundation of defense and creates setups for attack.
progression_path: Keeping push low over the net
Short push (two bounces on opponent side)
Push to set up third ball attack
common_mistakes: Bat angle too open — ball pops up high
Pushing from too far back — should be over the table
No wrist action — flat push with no spin
Pushing too hard causing ball to go long
Not reading incoming spin before pushing
pain_points: Popping the ball up giving opponent easy smash
Cannot keep pushes low and short
Reading spin on opponent's serve before pushing
Push has no backspin, easy for opponent to loop
drills: Short push to target zones (place coins on table)
Push and loop combination drill — push then attack next ball
Heavy backspin push practice — maximize spin
Push rally drill — 30 consecutive without error
Alternate short and long push to develop variation

skill_areas.name: Footwork
skill_areas.description: Side-to-side movement, in-out movement, and pivot footwork. Good footwork is the difference between average and excellent players.
progression_path: Ready position — weight on balls of feet
In-out movement (short ball to long ball)
common_mistakes: Standing flat-footed instead of on balls of feet
Crossing legs during side movement
Not returning to neutral position after each shot
Moving the arm instead of moving the feet to the ball
Taking too large steps — small, quick steps are better
pain_points: Standing too close or too far from table
Flat-footed and not ready to move
Cannot recover after wide forehand
Running out of time against fast rallies
drills: Falkenberg footwork drill (2 forehand 1 backhand pattern)
Side-to-side shuffle drill with multiball
In-out footwork with multiball — short ball then long ball
Shadow footwork without ball — practice movement patterns
Pivot drill — partner blocks to backhand, you pivot to forehand

skill_areas.name: Return of Serve
skill_areas.description: Reading spin, flicking short serves, and attacking long serves on return. The serve-receive battle decides most points at higher levels.
progression_path: Learn to watch contact point on opponent's serve
Basic push return against backspin serves
Touch/drop return to keep ball short
common_mistakes: Watching the ball instead of the opponent's contact point
Deciding what to do before reading the spin
Standing too far from table for short serve returns
Returning everything to the same spot — no variation
Panicking and just pushing everything back passively
pain_points: Cannot read spin type on serve
Returning into the net on heavy backspin serves
No attacking option on return
Getting aced by fast long serves
drills: Serve and return practice with partner — focus on reading spin
Banana flick return drill against short serves
Push return placement drill — return to specific zones
Loop return against long serves — partner serves long, you attack
Random serve receive — partner varies all serve types

skill_areas.name: Smash and Kill Shot
skill_areas.description: Finishing high balls with powerful flat hits and angled smashes. The exclamation mark at the end of a rally.
progression_path: Basic forehand smash against high balls
Angled smash — crosscourt and down the line
common_mistakes: Over-hitting — trying to kill every ball at maximum power
Poor timing — hitting too early or too late
Not moving feet to get into optimal position
Aiming for lines instead of safe targets
Tensing up before contact
pain_points: Over-hitting and missing the table
Poor timing on high balls
Lack of placement on smashes
Smash gets blocked back and no follow-up ready
drills: High ball smash practice with multiball feeder
Angled smash to corners drill — alternate sides
Counter-smash rally drill
Smash and recover drill — smash then play next ball
Target smash — place targets on table corners

skill_areas.name: Match Tactics & Strategy
skill_areas.description: Game plans against different playing styles. Understanding when to attack, when to control, and how to exploit opponent weaknesses.
progression_path: Understand your own playing style strengths
common_mistakes: No game plan — just reacting to everything
Not changing tactics when losing
Playing to opponent's strength instead of weakness
Ignoring the score situation — playing same way at 9-9 as at 1-1
Not using timeouts strategically
pain_points: Losing to players with unorthodox styles (choppers, pips)
Cannot adjust mid-match when plan is not working
No serve patterns — random serving
Losing close games due to mental pressure
drills: Practice serve-and-third-ball patterns — serve, predict return, attack
Play practice sets against different styles and review what worked
Watch match videos and identify tactical patterns
Practice specific patterns: serve short to forehand, attack weak return

tactical_guides.vs_choppers: Use heavy topspin loops with patience. Vary between spin and speed. Attack the crossover point. Don't try to blast through — build the rally. Choppers thrive on your impatience.
tactical_guides.vs_loopers: Keep ball short and low to deny looping opportunities. Attack first when possible. Use fast blocks to redirect speed. Target the middle/crossover.
tactical_guides.vs_penhold: Target the wide backhand (traditional penhold weakness). Use fast long serves to the backhand. Be prepared for strong forehand pivots.
tactical_guides.vs_pips_players: Understand spin reversal — your topspin comes back as backspin. Hit flat or with less spin to reduce reversal effect. Attack with speed rather than spin.

skill_areas.name: Mental Game & Psychology
skill_areas.description: Handling pressure, building confidence, and maintaining focus during matches. The mental side separates good players from great ones.
progression_path: Develop a pre-serve routine for consistency
Learn to reset after losing a point
common_mistakes: Dwelling on mistakes instead of focusing on next point
Changing your entire game when losing instead of making small adjustments
Getting frustrated with lucky nets and edges
Rushing between points when nervous
Negative body language affecting own confidence
pain_points: Choking at 9-9 or deuce situations
Playing much worse in tournaments than in practice
Losing to weaker players due to mental lapses
Cannot recover momentum after opponent takes a lead
drills: Play practice games starting from 8-8 to simulate pressure
Breathing routine between points — 3 seconds inhale, 3 exhale
Post-match journal — write what went well and what to improve
Visualization sessions — imagine yourself executing perfect shots
Play handicap games (start 0-5 down) to practice comeback mentality

skill_areas.name: Table Tennis Fitness
skill_areas.description: Off-table physical conditioning for speed, agility, endurance, and injury prevention specific to table tennis demands.
common_mistakes: Skipping warm-up before playing
Only doing upper body — neglecting legs and core
Overdoing wrist exercises leading to injury
Not stretching after sessions
Doing slow/heavy strength training instead of explosive/agility work
pain_points: Getting tired in long matches — poor endurance
Slow lateral movement despite good technique
Shoulder or elbow pain from overuse
Wrist fatigue during extended practice sessions
drills: Agility ladder side-shuffle drill — 3 sets of 30 seconds
Core rotation with medicine ball — mimics forehand loop motion
Wrist curls with light dumbbell (1-2kg) — 3 sets of 15
Interval sprints — 20 seconds sprint, 40 seconds rest, 8 rounds
Single-leg squats for balance and leg stability
Dynamic stretching warm-up routine (5 minutes before play)

skill_areas.name: Rubber Care & Maintenance
skill_areas.description: How to clean, protect, and maintain your rubbers to maximize lifespan and performance. Well-maintained rubbers grip better and last longer.
progression_path: Learn to apply protective film after every session
Clean rubber with spray cleaner and sponge
Store bat in a case away from heat/sunlight
Know when rubber needs replacing (loss of grip/spin)
Understand rubber aging — even unused rubbers degrade
common_mistakes: Leaving rubbers exposed to air/sunlight when not playing
Using water or household cleaners instead of proper rubber cleaner
Touching rubber surface with fingers (oil transfer)
Storing bat in a hot car or near windows
Not cleaning after every session
pain_points: Rubber loses grip too quickly
Expensive rubbers not lasting long enough
Not sure when to replace rubbers
Rubber surface becomes slippery or dull
drills: Post-session routine: clean with spray, apply film, store in case
Monthly grip test: place ball on rubber surface at 45 degrees — should not slide
Compare old vs new rubber — if spin generation drops noticeably, time to replace

skill_areas.name: Equipment Selection Guide
skill_areas.description: How to choose the right blade, rubber, and setup for your level and playing style. Avoid buying equipment that is too advanced or mismatched.
progression_path: Start with pre-assembled bat or basic all-wood blade + control rubbers
Understand blade speed vs control tradeoff
Learn about rubber types: inverted, short pips, long pips
Choose rubber thickness based on level
Upgrade to intermediate blade after 6-12 months
Match blade and rubber to your playing style
Advanced: understand carbon types (ALC, ZLC, ZL Fiber)
common_mistakes: Buying the most expensive/fastest equipment as a beginner
Copying pro player setups without having pro technique
Changing equipment too frequently — not giving time to adapt
Using maximum thickness rubber before technique is ready
Ignoring control rating and only looking at speed
pain_points: Overwhelmed by too many equipment choices
Wasted money on wrong equipment
Equipment feels too fast and uncontrollable
Not sure when to upgrade

absolute_beginner.option_1: Pre-assembled bat (Palio Expert 3.0 or Stiga Pro Carbon)
absolute_beginner.option_2: Stiga Allround Classic blade + Yasaka Mark V on both sides

intermediate_attacker.option_1: Stiga Clipper + DHS Hurricane 3 Neo (FH) + Xiom Vega Europe (BH)
intermediate_attacker.option_2: Yinhe N-11s + Tibhar Evolution MX-P (FH) + Xiom Vega Europe (BH)

advanced_attacker.option_2: Butterfly Timo Boll ALC + DHS Hurricane 3 Blue Sponge (FH) + Tenergy 05 (BH)

defender_chopper.option_1: Defensive blade + Tibhar Grass D.TecS (BH) + control rubber (FH)

skill_areas.name: Pimples (Pips) Rubber Play
skill_areas.description: Using and playing against short pimples and long pimples rubbers. Pips rubbers create unique spin effects that confuse opponents.
progression_path: Understand how pips reverse or negate spin
Short pips basics — flat hitting and blocking
Long pips basics — chopping and blocking
Spin reversal awareness — your topspin returns as backspin
Using pips to disrupt opponent's rhythm
common_mistakes: Trying to generate spin with long pips (they negate spin, not create it)
Not using pips' strengths — just pushing passively
Forgetting to twiddle at the right moment
Not understanding how opponent's spin affects your pips return
pain_points: Cannot control the ball with pips rubbers
Opponents figure out the pips game quickly
Difficulty switching between pips and inverted techniques
Not sure which pips type suits playing style
drills: Long pips chopping drill — focus on consistent spin reversal
Short pips flat hitting drill — punch through the ball
Twiddle drill — switch bat sides and play forehand with each rubber
Practice against pips — partner uses pips while you learn to read the spin

beginner.description: New players should learn proper grip (shakehand/penhold), basic strokes, consistent pushing, and good footwork habits. Start with controlled equipment.

intermediate.description: Players with consistent rallies should develop topspin attacks, strengthen backhand, and begin understanding tactical play. Upgrade equipment thoughtfully.

advanced.description: Competitive players must master serve-receive game, develop flick techniques, and refine tactical variations. Mental game becomes critical.

attacker.description: Aggressive topspin-dominant style. Close to mid-distance play with powerful forehand and backhand loops.
attacker.equipment_tendency: Fast offensive blade (ALC/ZLC) + spinny offensive rubbers (Tenergy, Dignics)

defender.description: Chopping and blocking style played from mid to far distance. Uses long pimples and spin variation to frustrate attackers.
defender.equipment_tendency: Defensive blade + long pimples (BH) + control/allround rubber (FH)

all_rounder.description: Balanced style combining controlled attacks with solid defense. Adaptable to different opponents.
all_rounder.equipment_tendency: All-wood or soft carbon blade + medium-speed rubbers (Vega, Evolution)

penhold_specialist.description: Penhold grip style — traditional (Chinese/Japanese) or reverse penhold backhand (RPB). Strong forehand with wristy serves.
penhold_specialist.equipment_tendency: Penhold blade (Chinese or Japanese) + tacky rubber (FH) + tensor or pips (BH)
